examples.title: Basic Turing Machine Simulator Structure
examples.description: Pseudo-code showing how a TM executes based on transition function
examples.code:
// TM = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, B, F)
// Configuration: (state, tape_position, tape_contents)

FUNCTION TM_Simulate(input_string):
 state ← q0
 tape ← InitializeTape(input_string) // Place input on tape
 head ← 0 // Start at leftmost cell
 
 WHILE state ∉ F AND transition_exists(state, tape[head]):
 current_symbol ← tape[head]
 (next_state, write_symbol, direction) ← δ(state, current_symbol)
 
 tape[head] ← write_symbol // Write new symbol
 
 IF direction = 'L' THEN
 head ← head - 1
 IF head < 0 THEN prepend_blank(tape); head ← 0
 ELSE IF direction = 'R' THEN
 head ← head + 1
 IF head >= length(tape) THEN append_blank(tape)
 END IF
 
 state ← next_state
 END WHILE
 
 IF state ∈ F THEN
 RETURN ACCEPT
 ELSE
 RETURN REJECT (or LOOP forever)
 END IF
examples.explanation: This simulates a basic single-tape deterministic TM. The loop continues until either a final state is reached (accept) or no valid transition exists (reject/halt). For NDTM, multiple branches would be explored.
examples.timeComplexity: Depends on TM and input - may not halt

examples.title: Multi-tape TM Simulation using Single-tape TM
examples.description: How to simulate a k-tape TM using a single tape TM
examples.explanation: This demonstrates that multi-tape TMs can be simulated by single-tape TMs, proving computational equivalence. The simulation uses multiple passes per original step.
examples.timeComplexity: O(T²) where T is original computation time
examples.spaceComplexity: O(T) where T is original tape usage

examples.title: Non-deterministic TM Simulation using Deterministic TM
examples.description: How to simulate NDTM using DTM with breadth-first search of configuration tree
examples.explanation: Shows NDTM simulation by DTM. BFS ensures all possible computation paths are explored. If any path accepts, the NDTM accepts. This proves NDTM and DTM have same computational power.
examples.timeComplexity: Exponential in worst case (exploring branching factor)

examples.title: Universal Turing Machine (UTM) Concept
examples.description: Conceptual structure of a UTM that can simulate any other TM
examples.explanation: This conceptual UTM shows how one machine can simulate any other. The key insight: the transition table of M is itself data that can be stored on tape and interpreted. This is the theoretical foundation of stored-program computers.
examples.timeComplexity: Overhead: O(T × |M|) where T = steps of simulated M, |M| = size of M
examples.spaceComplexity: Overhead: O(|M| + tape usage of M)
